content:
How one round trip of cryptography turns a readable postcard into a locked box — and why the padlock also proves who’s on the other end.

5 min read
Just now

--

You’re on coffee shop Wi-Fi. If a site uses plain HTTP, anyone on that network can read everything you send — your password, your messages, all of it, in plain text. One letter fixes that: the S in HTTPS, powered by a handshake that finishes in milliseconds.
This article walks that handshake message by message.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The heart of TLS 1.3 — two messages, one round trip, and both sides hold the same key.HTTP Is a Postcard
HTTP is the language browsers and servers speak, and it’s beautifully simple: the browser asks (`GET /page`), the server answers (`200 OK` plus the HTML).
The problem is how it travels. An HTTP message is a postcard: every router, every Wi-Fi hotspot, every network it crosses can read the whole thing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every hop between you and the server can read a plain-HTTP message.Worse: anyone in the middle can also *change* it. Inject an ad. Swap a download link for malware. The browser has no way to notice. HTTPS fixes this by putting the postcard inside a locked box — and the lock is a protocol called TLS.
What TLS Actually Promises
TLS — Transport Layer Security — sits between HTTP and the network. Same requests, same responses, but everything is wrapped before it leaves your machine.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

TLS slots in between HTTP and TCP/IP — the layers above and below don’t change.TLS makes three promises:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The three guarantees every HTTPS connection makes.- Encryption — outsiders see only scrambled bytes.
- Authentication— you’re talking to the real server, not an impostor.
- Integrity — if anyone tampers with the data in transit, both sides know instantly.
All three depend on one hard problem: you and the server need a shared secret key — and you’ve never met.
The Key Exchange Problem
Bulk encryption uses symmetric cryptography: one shared key that both locks and unlocks. It’s fast, but you can’t just mail that key across the network — an eavesdropper would simply copy it as it passes.
The trick is asymmetric cryptography. Each side generates a key pair — one public, one private — and here’s the magic: each side combines *its own private key* with *the other side’s public key*, and both computations land on the same value.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Both sides derive the same secret; the eavesdropper, holding only the two public halves, cannot.An attacker watching the wire sees both public keys — and still can’t compute the shared secret. That exchange is called Diffie-Hellman (in practice, its elliptic-curve variant, ECDHE), and it’s the engine of the handshake.
The TLS 1.3 Handshake, Step by Step
Two messages do almost all the work:
1. ClientHello — your browser sends the cipher suites it supports plus its public key share.
2. ServerHello — the server picks a cipher suite and returns its own key share. Both sides run Diffie-Hellman, and the shared secret exists — one round trip in.
Everything after this point is already encrypted. The server then proves its identity: it sends its certificate plus a signature only the certificate’s real owner could produce. The browser verifies both, the two sides exchange `Finished` messages, and every HTTP request from then on travels inside symmetric encryption.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The complete handshake — note the certificate is delivered inside the encrypted band, hidden from eavesdroppers.You can watch these exact messages with curl:
$ curl -vI https://example.com 2>&1 | grep TLS # output condensed
* TLSv1.3 (OUT), TLS handshake, Client hello (1):
* TLSv1.3 (IN), TLS handshake, Server hello (2):
* TLSv1.3 (IN), TLS handshake, Certificate (11):
* TLSv1.3 (IN), TLS handshake, Finished (20):
* TLSv1.3 (OUT), TLS handshake, Finished (20):
* SSL connection using TLSv1.3 / TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384Older TLS 1.2 needed two full round trips and sent the certificate in the clear before encryption started. TLS 1.3 cut the handshake in half and pulled the certificate inside the encrypted channel.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

TLS 1.2 vs 1.3 — half the round trips, and the certificate is no longer visible on the wire.Total cost: one round trip — a few dozen milliseconds. And on a reconnect, TLS 1.3 can resume with zero: both sides reuse a session ticket from last time and skip straight to encrypted data (0-RTT).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

First connection costs one round trip; resumption with a session ticket can cost none.Why Trust the Certificate?
One gap remains. An attacker sitting between you and the server could run Diffie-Hellman with each side separately and quietly relay traffic. Encryption alone doesn’t prove who’s on the other end.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Without authentication, an impostor could handshake with both sides at once.That’s the certificate’s job. It binds the server’s public key to its domain name, and it’s signed by a certificate authority (CA) — an organization your browser already trusts.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The certificate ties a public key to a domain, and the CA’s signature vouches for that binding.Your operating system ships with a list of trusted root CAs. If the certificate’s signature chain traces back to that list, the padlock appears; if not, you get the full-page red warning.
$ openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -brief
CONNECTION ESTABLISHED
Protocol version: TLSv1.3
Ciphersuite: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
Verification: OK # ← the chain traced back to a trusted rootPress enter or click to view image in full size

The chain of trust — server certificate → intermediate CA → a root CA your OS already holds.The impostor can’t forge a CA signature over their own key, so the man in the middle is locked out.
- Plain HTTP is a postcard: readable and editable by every network hop in between.
- TLS wraps HTTP with three guarantees — encryption, authentication, integrity.
- Diffie-Hellman lets two strangers build a shared secret in public: private key + other side’s public key = same value on both ends, uncomputable for eavesdroppers.
- TLS 1.3 finishes the handshake in one round trip (~30 ms), delivers the certificate already encrypted, and can resume later connections with zero round trips.
- The certificate, signed by a CA your OS trusts, is what stops a man-in-the-middle — encryption without authentication wouldn’t be enough.
Prefer the animated version? The full video plays every one of these diagrams in motion:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OKVmJp2rls0
